Title: The biosynthesis and nutritional uses of carotenoids

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Capsicum annuum; Brassica napus; Solanum lycopersicum
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associates with sequestration of carotenoids in chromoplast fibrils and contributes to storage capacity
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Association with chromoplast fibrils and carotenoid sequestration studies
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Fibrillin is associated with carotenoid sequestration in pepper chromoplast fibrils. 2. Sequestration is an important regulatory layer for carotenoid accumulation. 3. Altered carotenoid content can modify plastid ultrastructure in transgenic crops.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve carotenoid storage stability and sink strength rather than biosynthetic flux.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review states fibrillin was the only sequestration-associated gene isolated at the time.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Non-functional HPPD indirectly causes phytoene accumulation by limiting plastoquinone required for desaturation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HPPD is required for plastoquinone and tocopherol formation. 2. HPPD deficiency indirectly blocks phytoene desaturation and causes phytoene accumulation. 3. This links carotenoid desaturation to plastoquinone metabolism.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is an indirect carotenoid-related gene rather than a core biosynthetic enzyme.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Non-functional redox-chain component causes phytoene accumulation by preventing desaturation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Alternative oxidase/redox-chain defects impair carotenoid desaturation. 2. Arabidopsis and tomato mutants accumulate phytoene as a consequence. 3. This demonstrates dependence of desaturation on plastid redox balance.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review discusses Arabidopsis and tomato ghost mutants as evidence for electron transport involvement, but without a strong focus on a single gene name.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses pyruvate-derived hydroxyethyl-TPP with glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to form DXP
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provitamin A source; antioxidant; human health benefits including eye health
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides precursor supply for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; complementation and/or in vitro enzyme activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXPS catalyzes the first step of the MEP pathway producing DXP. 2. This step contributes plastidial IPP/DMAPP supply for carotenoid biosynthesis. 3. Multiple plant orthologs were identified, including Arabidopsis copies.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DXP also feeds vitamin B1 and B6 biosynthesis; Arabidopsis has three orthologs, but only DXPS-2 functionality was shown at the time.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream target to increase carotenoid precursor supply, but broad isoprenoid effects require caution.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Rearranges and reduces DXP to MEP
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports precursor formation for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression and enzyme activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXR catalyzes the first unique committed reaction after DXP in the MEP route. 2. The enzyme requires NADPH and Mn2+. 3. Plant genes were functionally identified.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible precursor-supply engineering target for carotenoids and other plastid isoprenoids.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers CMP to MEP to form CDP-ME
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports IPP/DMAPP production upstream of carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: E. coli expression and activity assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MCT catalyzes conversion of MEP to CDP-ME in the MEP pathway. 2. Arabidopsis enzyme was functionally identified. 3. This step contributes to carotenoid precursor supply.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NA

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates CDP-ME to CDP-MEP
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports precursor flow into carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology and heterologous expression/activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CMK catalyzes phosphorylation of CDP-ME in the MEP pathway. 2. Plant homologs from tomato and mint were functionally characterized. 3. This step is part of plastidial precursor supply for carotenoid formation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NA

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts CDP-MEP to methylerythritol cyclodiphosphate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to upstream precursor biosynthesis for carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology-based identification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MCS is part of the MEP pathway leading to IPP/DMAPP. 2. Plant evidence in this review was primarily homology-based. 3. It functions upstream of carotenoid synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not functionally characterized in plants in detail in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts ME-cPP to HMBPP in the MEP pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports plastid isoprenoid precursor synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional assignment from Arabidopsis gene identification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HDS catalyzes a late MEP-pathway step toward IPP/DMAPP formation. 2. Arabidopsis GcpE was identified as the corresponding gene. 3. It contributes to precursor supply for carotenoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NA

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts HMBPP to a mixture of IPP and DMAPP
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates immediate C5 precursors for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology-based assignment
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IDS was proposed to form IPP and DMAPP from HMBPP. 2. Plant evidence was inferred from bacterial comparison and Arabidopsis homology. 3. It lies immediately upstream of GGPP synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not functionally characterized in plants in this review; assignment based on comparison with bacterial enzymes.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Tomato; pepper; daffodil; Arabidopsis; Clarkia breweri; marigold
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reversibly isomerizes IPP and DMAPP
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides allylic prenyl precursor needed for GGPP and phytoene formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification of plastidic enzyme; gene identification by homology and plastid-targeting sequence
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plastidic IPPI interconverts IPP and DMAPP for carotenoid precursor synthesis. 2. Enzyme was purified from tomato, pepper and daffodil. 3. Plastid-targeted genes were identified in several species.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Distinguished from cytosolic IPP isomerase forms by N-terminal plastid-targeting extensions.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Pepper; tomato; Arabidopsis; Catharanthus roseus; sunflower; marigold; Lupinus albus
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses prenyl diphosphate units to generate GGPP
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates immediate C20 precursor for phytoene synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme purification; cDNA isolation; plastidial isoenzyme identification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGPPS generates GGPP, the immediate precursor of carotenoids. 2. Plastidial forms were purified from pepper and partially purified from tomato. 3. Arabidopsis contains a multigene family with plastidial isoenzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Some identified GGPPS family members may serve non-carotenoid plastid isoprenoid pathways.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; maize; melon; carrot; grapefruit; unshiu; daffodil; rice; pepper; marigold; soybean; tobacco; tomato; Gentiana lutea
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two GGPP molecules via prephytoene diphosphate to form phytoene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes carotenoid accumulation and strongly controls pathway flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; biochemical characterization; heterologous expression; transgenic overexpression and antisense studies; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as the major control step / first committed step, especially PSY-1 in tomato fruit
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PSY catalyzes the first committed step of carotenoid formation from GGPP. 2. Tomato PSY-1 exerts the greatest control over pathway flux during fruit ripening. 3. Engineering PSY can strongly alter carotenoid content but may trigger pleiotropic effects or co-suppression depending on promoter and gene choice.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dwarfism, altered gibberellin and ABA levels, ectopic pigmentation, co-suppression/gene silencing when endogenous Psy-1 is constitutively expressed.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target for carotenoid biofortification; use tissue-specific promoters and non-homologous/synthetic PSY to avoid pleiotropic effects and silencing.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tomato and tobacco each have two PSY genes; Gentiana has four; membrane association and redox/membrane context may regulate activity.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Pepper; Arabidopsis; tomato; maize; daffodil; rice; soybean; tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces double bonds into phytoene to produce phytofluene and then ζ-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for conversion of colorless phytoene toward colored carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; plastid assays; purified enzyme from pepper; heterologous expression
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not the strongest control point compared with PSY-1 in tomato fruit
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDS catalyzes the first desaturation reactions from phytoene toward ζ-carotene. 2. It is membrane-bound and uses FAD/quinones. 3. Expression increases during tomato fruit ripening and chemical inhibition or redox defects cause phytoene accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Desaturation depends on plastoquinone/redox chain; HPPD deficiency or alternative oxidase defects indirectly block this step.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Pepper; Arabidopsis; maize; soybean; daffodil; tomato
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Lycopene via neurosporene and poly-cis intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Desaturates ζ-carotene through neurosporene toward lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for lycopene formation from ζ-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; heterologous expression of pepper enzyme; in vitro assays
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZDS catalyzes later desaturation steps from ζ-carotene to lycopene. 2. The enzyme has broad geometric isomer specificity and requires oxygen/plastoquinones. 3. It works with CRTISO to produce all-trans lycopene in plants.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Shows 33-35% similarity to PDS.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts poly-cis lycopene intermediates to all-trans lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables efficient cyclization and normal downstream carotenoid formation; mutation causes poly-cis lycopene accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Map-based cloning of tomato tangerine mutant; co-expression in E. coli; Arabidopsis mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRTISO was identified as the gene defective in tomato tangerine. 2. It converts prolycopene to all-trans lycopene, enabling subsequent cyclisation. 3. Light can partially bypass the need for enzymatic isomerization in green tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to alter lycopene isomer composition and fruit color.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Green tissues may rely on photoisomerization, reducing visible mutant phenotype under light.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces one or two β-rings into lycopene to form γ-carotene and β-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes β-carotene accumulation and decreases lycopene proportion
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional complementation in E. coli; mutant analysis; transgenic expression/antisense in tomato
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Branch-switch enzyme rather than global rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCYB catalyzes β-ring formation from lycopene and directs flux to β-carotene. 2. Tomato fruit-specific CYC-B underlies Beta/old gold phenotypes and strongly alters fruit carotenoid composition. 3. Transgenic expression in tomato raises β-carotene, while antisense raises lycopene modestly.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target to shift lycopene-rich tissues toward provitamin A β-carotene; useful in tomato and Golden Rice engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tomato possesses a chromoplast-specific cyclase, CYC-B, distinct from LCYB.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: δ-carotene; with LCYB leads to α-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces an ε-ring into lycopene to form δ-carotene, enabling α-branch formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directs flux into the ε-branch and affects balance between β-carotene and lutein-related products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation and functional complementation in E. coli; mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCYE forms a single ε-ring and controls branch allocation toward α-carotene/lutein biosynthesis. 2. Its inability to introduce two ε-rings in most species helps regulate cyclic carotenoid composition. 3. Tomato Delta mutant reflects altered LCYE function.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Lettuce LCYE is exceptional in being able to introduce two ε-rings for lactucaxanthin formation.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Di-iron oxygenase-like membrane hydroxylase with histidine clusters
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates β-rings of β-carotene to form β-cryptoxanthin and zeaxanthin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes conversion of β-carotene to xanthophylls; in co-transformed tomato enables zeaxanthin production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression; transgenic co-transformation in tomato
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. β-carotene hydroxylases introduce hydroxyl groups into β-rings to make zeaxanthin pathway products. 2. Pepper hydroxylases are ferredoxin-dependent and membrane-associated. 3. Co-expression with β-LCY enabled zeaxanthin engineering in tomato fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering zeaxanthin-rich crops when adequate β-carotene precursor pool is available.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No ε-ring 3-hydroxylase had been identified at the time; this limited full elucidation of lutein synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epoxidizes zeaxanthin to violaxanthin via antheraxanthin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts zeaxanthin into violaxanthin; suppression increases zeaxanthin accumulation in potato tubers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression for pepper ZEP; antisense/sense suppression in transgenic potato
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZEP catalyzes the epoxidation side of the violaxanthin cycle. 2. Its suppression in potato increases zeaxanthin and total carotenoids. 3. The gene product belongs to the lipocalin family.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering target for zeaxanthin enrichment in storage organs.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Despite impact on an ABA precursor step, the potato study reported no obvious phytohormone imbalance phenotype.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: De-epoxidizes violaxanthin back to zeaxanthin via antheraxanthin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mediates reversible de-epoxidation within the violaxanthin cycle
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification and gene identification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VDE catalyzes the reverse arm of the violaxanthin cycle. 2. The enzyme requires ascorbate and MGDG and is active at acidic pH. 3. Plant genes were identified from several species.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Relevant to photoprotection as well as nutritional carotenoid composition.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts violaxanthin to neoxanthin with formation of an allenic end group
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for neoxanthin formation, an ABA precursor carotenoid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation and functional testing from potato and tomato
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NXS forms neoxanthin from violaxanthin. 2. Tomato NXS sequence is identical to CYC-B, suggesting possible bifunctionality. 3. No Arabidopsis NXS had been identified at the time.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functional assignment is unusual because tomato NXS and CYC-B are identical at the deduced amino acid sequence level.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 5,6-epoxy xanthophylls into pepper-specific ketocarotenoids containing a cyclopentane ring
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces red pepper-specific ketocarotenoids in ripe fruit; viral expression in tobacco leaves generated capsanthin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification from pepper chromoplasts; functional enzyme characterization; viral vector expression in Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCS produces capsanthin and capsorubin in ripe pepper fruits. 2. The enzyme is closely related to LCYB and also shows cyclase activity. 3. Viral-vector expression demonstrated the feasibility of producing capsanthin in heterologous tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for producing high-value carotenoids in plant biofactories or specialty crops.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These ketocarotenoids are largely unique to ripe pepper fruit.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Crocus sativus
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cleaves 9-cis epoxycarotenoids to produce xanthoxin, a precursor of ABA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels carotenoids into ABA biosynthesis through oxidative cleavage
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; gene identification; in vitro and complementation-based substrate testing
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Maize VP14 cleaves 9-cis-violaxanthin and 9-cis-neoxanthin to cis-xanthoxin. 2. Arabidopsis contains a family of related cleavage genes. 3. Cleavage enzyme substrate specificity appears broader in vitro than likely in vivo.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes uncertainty between in vitro substrate breadth and true in vivo function.

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Common carotenoid accumulation regulator / chromoplast differentiation factor
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promising regulator associated with orange curd and increased carotenoid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Promising but not yet fully established regulator in this review
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Or was identified in orange cauliflower with elevated carotenoid accumulation. 2. It is highlighted as a promising regulatory gene for carotenoid formation. 3. No plant carotenoid regulatory genes had been fully isolated at the time.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review explicitly states no regulatory genes involved in carotenoid formation had yet been isolated; Or is mentioned as promising rather than established.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Indirect control of carotenoid accumulation during fruit development
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: hp-2/DET1 light signaling mutation increases carotenoid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent indirect regulator via light signaling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tomato hp-2 corresponds to a light signaling mutation in DET1. 2. The mutation increases carotenoid content. 3. The effect is indirect rather than a dedicated carotenoid-pathway regulator.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is a core summarized gene in carotenoid-accumulation breeding examples, but it is not a direct pathway TF.
